planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown clumps in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade in spring; Allegheny spurge is slower to spread than Japanese pachysandra but far more beautiful, with ornamentally mottled foliage.

directions: Plant divisions or container specimens in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; Allegheny spurge is the native North American alternative to the ubiquitous Japanese pachysandra, producing semi-evergreen to evergreen mats of large, attractively silver-mottled leaves that are significantly more ornamental than the plain green of P. terminalis. The lightly fragrant, white to pink flower spikes emerge at ground level in early spring before the new leaves expand, adding a subtle bonus to the elegant foliage. It spreads more slowly than its Japanese counterpart but colonizes effectively over 3–5 years in suitable conditions. Extremely useful for naturalizing dry to moist, heavily shaded areas in zone 4–9 gardens, including the Gulf South where it handles summer heat better than expected for a woodland native.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in early fall or container-grown plants in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Solomon's seal is long-lived and expands slowly but steadily to form elegant colonies in undisturbed woodland settings.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in early fall or container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Solomon's seal is a native eastern North American woodland perennial with one of the most distinctive and elegant structures in the shade garden — arching, unbranched stems set with paired, smooth, alternate, oval leaves along the entire length, with small pendant clusters of white, bell-shaped flowers hanging from the leaf axils in late spring, followed by blue-black berries. The plant expands steadily by rhizomes to form handsome woodland colonies. Spectacular fall color is a bonus — stems turn clear golden yellow as they die back. The variegated selection P. odoratum 'Variegatum' (creamy-white leaf margins, fragrant flowers) is widely grown. The thick rhizomes were used as food and medicine by many indigenous nations.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in early fall in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; merrybells is slow to establish from division but is virtually permanent once settled and requires no maintenance in woodland garden conditions.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in early fall in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; merrybells (large-flowered bellwort) is one of the loveliest native spring ephemerals of eastern North American woodlands, producing arching stems with twisted, perfoliate leaves (appearing to be threaded onto the stem) hung with one or two drooping, narrow, butter-yellow, bell-shaped flowers in mid-spring. Plants emerge early, bloom briefly and beautifully, then continue producing attractive foliage through summer before going dormant in late summer. They naturalize slowly from division or scattered seed to form small, graceful colonies in woodland gardens. Excellent combined with spring ephemeral companions like trillium, hepatica, and trout lily. Rarely browsed by deer.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; blue mistflower spreads aggressively by rhizomes in moist, fertile conditions and should be sited where its spreading habit can be accommodated.

directions: Plant divisions in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; blue mistflower (hardy ageratum) is a native eastern North American perennial that blooms in late summer to fall — precisely when the shade garden most needs color — producing frothy, powder-blue to lavender flower heads in large flat-topped clusters that closely resemble annual ageratum and are magnets for migrating monarch butterflies, painted ladies, and dozens of native bee species. It spreads vigorously by rhizomes in moist, fertile soil and is best used in naturalistic plantings, rain gardens, and woodland edges where its colonizing habit is a virtue. Unlike many fall-blooming natives, it tolerates considerable shade. Excellent for the critical late-season pollinator support corridor in southeastern and Gulf South gardens.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination, or plant container-grown divisions in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; blue-eyed grass naturalizes readily by self-seeding in suitable moist, open conditions.

directions: Direct sow in fall or plant container divisions in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; blue-eyed grass is a charming native member of the iris family that looks like a delicate grass but produces a continuous succession of tiny, six-petaled, bright violet-blue flowers with a yellow center from late spring through summer in a cheerful, prolific display. The flat, grass-like foliage is neat and attractive at all times. It naturalizes readily in moist meadow, prairie, and lawn-edge conditions by self-seeding to form soft, spreading colonies over time. Excellent at the front of a border, in rock gardens, along stream banks, and scattered through a native lawn mix. Highly attractive to small native bees and hoverflies. The related S. bermudiana (Bermuda blue-eyed grass) is similar but slightly more tolerant of dry conditions.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
